title: Using finished compost as mushroom-growing substrate
subtitle: Preparing spent compost and coffee grounds for oyster and king oyster mushroom cultivation
excerpt: Finished compost and spent coffee grounds provide the nutrients and moisture-holding capacity oyster mushrooms need. A 5-litre batch of inoculated substrate fits in a small bucket and produces multiple flushes over six to eight weeks. This is the substrate preparation, inoculation, and fruiting sequence using garden compost rather than commercial substrate.
sourceNotes: Field & Forest Products mycelium growing guides (public domain educational content). Tradd Cotter 'Organic Mushroom Farming and Mycoremediation' (referenced for mechanism context, not verbatim). Fun Guys Farm UK growing guides. RHS guidance on growing mushrooms in raised beds.

projectSchedule.title: Check mycelium colonisation
projectSchedule.body: After two weeks, the substrate should show visible white mycelium threads throughout. A fully colonised bucket looks uniformly pale with white filaments visible through any translucent walls. If only partial colonisation is visible, give it another week before initiating fruiting.

projectSchedule.title: First flush expected
projectSchedule.body: Once fully colonised and moved to a cool, humid spot with indirect light, the first pins (tiny mushroom primordia) should appear within three to seven days. Keep humidity high by misting the fruiting surface with clean water twice daily.

content.text: Oyster mushrooms are wood-decomposing fungi that feed on lignin and cellulose. Finished garden compost, especially compost with some wood-chip content, works as a growing substrate. Adding spent coffee grounds improves the nitrogen content and gives the mycelium a richer growing medium. This mix costs less than commercial straw and produces similar yields.
Gather finished, sieved compost: no large pieces of undecomposed wood or root material. The compost should smell earthy, not ammonia-like. Total volume: 3-4 litres.
Add spent coffee grounds: 1-1.5 litres of freshly spent grounds (used within 24 hours to minimise green-mould contamination). Mix thoroughly with the compost.

Heat the mix. Put it in a heatproof pot. Pour near-boiling water over it to reach about 65-70°C. Hold that heat for 1 hour on a low hob or in a low oven. This step kills rival moulds. Do not use a pressure cooker. High-pressure heat kills the soil life the spawn feeds on.
Allow the substrate to cool to below 25°C before inoculating. Hot substrate kills the mycelium in the spawn.
Buy oyster mushroom grain spawn from a reputable UK supplier. 200-300 g of grain spawn is sufficient for 5 litres of substrate. Break the spawn up into individual grains before adding it.

Layer substrate and spawn in a 5-10 litre bucket with drainage holes drilled in the base and one side. Alternate 50 mm layers of substrate with thin layers of spawn. Finish with a substrate layer on top.
Cover the top loosely with polythene or a lid. This stops the mix drying out. Leave a gap for air: CO2 builds up fast in sealed buckets and slows the mycelium.
During colonisation: keep the bucket at 18-24°C, out of direct sunlight. Visible white mycelium threads should appear within five to seven days and colonise the full substrate in 10-18 days.

To start fruiting: move the bucket to a cooler spot (12-18°C) with indirect light. Raise humidity by misting the substrate top twice daily with clean water. Pins normally appear at the drainage holes and top surface within three to seven days of the change.

Harvest each cluster when the cap edges are still slightly curled inward. Cut at the base of the cluster rather than pulling, to avoid disturbing the mycelium. One bucket typically gives three to four flushes over six to eight weeks, with the first flush being the largest.

attrs.symptom: Green or black mould appearing on the substrate surface
attrs.cause: Trichoderma or Aspergillus contamination, usually from insufficient pasteurisation or introducing spawn before the substrate cooled fully
attrs.fix: Remove visibly contaminated sections immediately. If contamination covers more than 30% of the surface, the bucket is compromised. Improve pasteurisation time and ensure substrate temperature is below 22°C before next inoculation.

attrs.symptom: Mushrooms are elongated with small caps (etiolated growth)
attrs.cause: Too much CO2 and insufficient fresh air exchange during fruiting
attrs.fix: Fan the bucket once or twice daily to exchange air, or move to a room with better ventilation.
